title: Programmable cavity-enhanced telecom quantum memory in thin-film lithium niobate
authors: Chengdong Yang, Hanwen Guo, Yu-Yang An, Qian He, Chi Lu, Ziheng Jiang, Yan-Qing Lu, Shining Zhu, Xiao-Song Ma
abstract: Spectrally multiplexed telecom quantum networks require quantum memories that combine efficient storage with programmable frequency addressing. An ideal integrated implementation should therefore unite a native telecom transition, efficient storage and fast on-chip spectral control. Here we demonstrate a cavity-enhanced quantum memory in an isotopically purified $^{167}\mathrm{Er}^{3+}$-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator. Long-lived hyperfine shelving states support persistent, high-contrast atomic frequency comb preparation, with a single-component comb lifetime of $277.6 \pm 52.6$s. Together with cavity impedance matching, this yields an on-chip storage efficiency of $23.3 \pm 0.5\%$ for 100-ns storage. The intrinsic electro-optic response of lithium niobate enables frequency-selective storage and routing of retrieved photons at rates up to 20~MHz with inter-channel crosstalk below $10^{-4}$. We further store and retrieve time-energy-entangled telecom photons, violating an entanglement-witness bound by more than 11 standard deviations and thus verifying the quantum nature of the storage process. Our results establish erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate as a programmable light--matter interface for spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.
summary: This paper presents a programmable quantum memory using erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate that integrates efficient photon storage with high-speed electro-optic control. By leveraging the Pockels effect, the device can rapidly route telecom-wavelength photons, enabling the development of scalable, spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.
main_problem: The need for integrated, high-efficiency, and programmable quantum memories at telecom wavelengths that can support spectrally multiplexed quantum networks.
main_result: Demonstration of a cavity-enhanced quantum memory in erbium-doped thin-film lithium niobate that achieves 23.3% storage efficiency and enables high-speed (20 MHz) frequency-selective routing of entangled photons.
method: The researchers used the Atomic Frequency Comb (AFC) protocol in an isotopically purified 167Er3+-doped thin-film lithium niobate microring resonator, utilizing the electro-optic effect for fast frequency control and verifying entanglement via a Franson interferometer.
model_or_system: An isotopically purified 167Er3+-doped thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) microring resonator operating at cryogenic temperatures (260 mK) and high magnetic fields (1.855 T).
key_observables: Storage efficiency, AFC lifetime, inter-channel crosstalk, optical coherence time (T2), entanglement witness (W), and second-order cross-correlation function g2(0).
important_parameters: Wavelength ~1532 nm, storage efficiency of 23.3%, AFC lifetime of 277.6 s, electro-optic tuning efficiency of 1.11 GHz/V, and modulation rates up to 20 MHz.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the device layout, SEM, and optical characterization; Figure 3 illustrates high-speed routing pulse sequences and frequency-selective histograms; Figure 4 details the entanglement storage setup and verification results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the device architecture and material properties, describes the spectral preparation and cavity characterization, demonstrates high-speed electro-optic routing, and concludes with the storage and retrieval of time-energy-entangled photons.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates a scalable, integrated platform for controlling light-matter interactions and managing quantum correlations in a multiplexed network.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Device layout and optical characterization of the thin-film lithium niobate (TFLN) quantum memory. (A) Schematic of the 167Er3+-doped TFLN device. (B) Scanning electron microscopy image of the device. (C) Cross-sectional schematic of the device structure. (D) Optical transmission spectrum (red curve) and fluorescence spectrum (blue curve) of the device. Inset: close-up of the resonance studied in this work. The shaded area indicates the 202-GHz inhomogeneous broadening of the erbium ions. (E) Power-dependent transmission spectra. (F) The loaded quality factor (Qloaded) and extinction ratio (ER) extracted from Fano fits as a function of the input power, revealing the saturation...

FIG. 2. Persistent atomic frequency combs and quantum storage efficiency. (A) Normalized hole depth as a function of wait time. The single-exponential fit yields an AFC lifetime of 277.6 ± 52.6 s. Inset: Experimental pulse sequence. (B) Normalized transmission spectrum of AFC (red solid line) prepared at a frequency modulation (FM) amplitude of 2 MHz. An unpumped probe scan (grey dashed line) confirms that the readout does not perturb the AFC. (C) Storage efficiency (red circles), theoretical efficiency curve (blue solid line), coupling parameter K (green dashed line), and effective cooperativity C′

FIG. 3. High-speed routing of the quantum memory. (A) Experimental pulse sequence. Distinct DC voltages (Vn) shift the cavity resonance to target channels to prepare independent AFCs (fn). During the storage phase, high-speed voltage modulation dynamically routes the retrieved photons. (B) Time-resolved histograms for input photons fixed at f1 under square- wave voltage modulations. Echoes are retrieved exclusively during V1 segments, while pulses falling under V2 are suppressed. (C) Same measurement as in (B) but with the input photon frequency fixed at f2, demonstrating frequency-selective dynamic routing complementary to (B). (D) Zoomed view of the f2 input data at TEO = 50 ns,...

FIG. 4. Storage of time-energy entangled telecom photons. (A) AFC preparation module. (B) Integrated entan- gled photon-pair source based on a silicon nitride dual Mach–Zehnder interferometer (DMZI) microring resonator. (C) The 167Er3+:TFLN quantum memory. (D) Entanglement analyzer consisting of two unbalanced asymmetric Mach–Zehnder inter- ferometers (AMZIs). (E) Frequency alignment between the memory cavity and the input signal photon. The clear dip in the signal counts (S.C.) indicates that the cavity resonance has been electro-optically tuned into resonance with the fixed signal wavelength, while the idler counts remain constant as expected.
